title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Two Wheels, One City: The Urban Cycling Revolution
content.text: In Copenhagen, more than sixty per cent of residents cycle to work every day, in all weathers, dressed in ordinary clothes rather than lycra. The bicycle is not a lifestyle choice there; it is simply the most practical way to move around the city. (37) [37] Esta normalidad en torno a la bicicleta es el resultado de décadas de inversión pública y cambio cultural. [/37] It was not always so: in the 1960s, Copenhagen was as car-dominated as any other Western city, and the cycling infrastructure was built up deliberately over the following decades. || Other cities are following different paths toward the same destination. Amsterdam, Utrecht, Seville and Bogotá have all made cycling central to their transport strategies. (38) [38] Cada ciudad ha adaptado su enfoque según su geografía, su clima y las necesidades específicas de sus ciudadanos. [/38] What they share is a recognition that safe, connected cycling networks are not a luxury but a public health and environmental necessity. || The case for cycling in cities rests on compelling evidence. Regular cycling reduces the risk of cardiovascular disease, type-2 diabetes and certain cancers. (39) [39] Además, los ciclistas urbanos llegan a su destino en tiempos comparables o mejores que los conductores de automóvil en las horas punta. [/39] Studies in London and New York have found that for journeys under ten kilometres, cycling is frequently the fastest mode of transport available. || Infrastructure is the crucial variable. Cycling rates in cities with protected, segregated lanes are dramatically higher than in those that rely on painted road markings or shared paths. (40) [40] La percepción de seguridad es el factor más determinante a la hora de animar a nuevos ciclistas a usar la bicicleta. [/40] Research consistently shows that the single greatest barrier to cycling is not fitness or distance but fear of traffic. || Bike-sharing schemes have lowered the threshold for urban cycling still further. Dockless bicycles and e-bikes available via smartphone apps have made cycling accessible to people who cannot afford or maintain their own bicycle. (41) [41] Las bicicletas eléctricas, en particular, han atraído a personas mayores y a quienes se enfrentan a rutas con pendientes pronunciadas. [/41] Usage data from schemes in Paris, London and Beijing shows that e-bikes have recruited large numbers of cyclists who had never previously ridden regularly in the city. || The cultural shift required to make a city truly cycle-friendly is as important as any piece of infrastructure. (42) [42] Cambiar la percepción del automóvil como símbolo de estatus es quizás el mayor desafío a largo plazo. [/42] But as Copenhagen demonstrates, once that shift takes hold, it tends to be self-reinforcing: more cyclists make roads feel safer, which attracts still more cyclists, in a virtuous cycle that reshapes the city.

paragraphs.A: This normalisation of cycling is the product of decades of deliberate public investment and cultural change.
paragraphs.B: Each city has adapted its approach to its own geography, climate and the specific needs of its residents.
paragraphs.C: The perceived safety of the cycling environment is the most decisive factor in encouraging new riders.
paragraphs.D: Electric bikes in particular have attracted older riders and those facing hilly routes who would not otherwise cycle.
paragraphs.E: Changing the perception of the car as a status symbol may be the greatest long-term challenge.
paragraphs.F: Cyclists also tend to reach their destinations in comparable or shorter times than car drivers during peak hours.
paragraphs.G: Some cities have introduced congestion charges that have significantly reduced motor traffic in their centres.

questions.explanation: A explains that Copenhagen's cycling normality is the result of deliberate investment, connecting with the historical description of the 1960s.
B notes that each city adapts its approach according to its conditions, before the statement about what all of them share.
F adds the argument of cycling's time efficiency during rush hour, preparing the studies from London and New York.
C introduces the perception of safety as a key factor, directly linking to studies on fear of traffic.
D specifies the appeal of e-bikes for new groups of users, connecting with data from Paris, London and Beijing.
E points out the cultural shift needed regarding the car as a status symbol, preparing the conclusion with the Copenhagen example.
